journal-title-group.journal-title: International journal of hyperthermia : the official journal of European Society for Hyperthermic Oncology, North American Hyperthermia Group

title-group.article-title: Histotripsy of the Hepatic Capsule and the Gastric Wall: Acute Treatment Effects in a Swine Model

article-meta.aff: 2Cleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine; Cleveland Clinic
3Department of Pathobiology, School of Veterinary Medicine, University of Pennsylvania
4Department of Medical Physics, University of Wisconsin
5Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Wisconsin
6Department of Biomedical Engineering, Virginia Technologic University

author-notes.corresp: Address correspondence to: Timothy J. Ziemlewicz MD, 600 Highland Avenue, Madison, WI 53792, tziemlewicz@uwhealth.org

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Purpose:
sec.p: To evaluate the safety of histotripsy at the hepatic capsule when the treatment zone extends into the gastric wall in a live porcine model.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: Spherical 2.5 cm histotripsy treatments (n=12 treatments) were performed in the livers of six swine with treatment margins extending ~5 mm into the adjacent gastric wall. Each animal received two treatments: One each with a gas-filled and collapsed stomach. Post-procedural contrast-enhanced CT was performed prior to euthanasia, necropsy, and gross and histopathology.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: Histotripsy of the liver and adjacent stomach was technically successful in all cases (12/12). There was no statistically significant difference in treatment zone size when the stomach was inflated or collapsed. Gross pathologic evidence of gastric injury was noted in 12/12 subjects without evidence of perforation or gastric leak. CT evidence of gastric injury was observed in 10/12 treatments without evidence of gastric perforation or leakage noted (12/12). Stomach histopathology confirmed injuries as partial (non-transmural) in 11/12 cases and complete (transmural) in 1/12 cases (collapsed stomach).

sec.title: Conclusions:
sec.p: Histotripsy performed at the hepatic capsule with treatment margins extending into the stomach generally results in minor, partial thickness gastric injuries, which are not worsened by the presence of gas. Full thickness injuries are however possible and caution should be exercised to minimize the amount of stomach wall overlap.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Histotripsy is an emerging non-invasive, non-thermal, focused ultrasound-based therapy recently achieved FDA approval for the treatment of primary and metastatic liver tumors. High-amplitude ultrasound pulses create cavitation at a focal point and the subsequent rapid expansion and collapse of gas bubbles results in mechanical destruction of targeted tissue (1). Recent live animal work has demonstrated a lack of bleeding after transcapsular histotripsy in both normal and anticoagulated animals, expanding the locations and conditions under which histotripsy can be applied in the liver (2). Tumors in the left hepatic lobe are particularly good candidates for histotripsy due to an ultrasound window unobstructed by ribs (3). However, the left lobe of the liver is also adjacent to portions of the stomach which could be injured if included in the treatment prescription or if the stomach moves into the treatment zone during application of histotripsy.

To date, physicians performing histotripsy of the liver have deliberately avoided the inclusion of stomach and bowel in the prescribed treatment zone (4,5). In ex vivo studies, histotripsy can cause damage to the small bowel when included in the treatment zone but having the beam path traverse gas-filled small bowel when targeting deeper structures may be safe (6). Compared to the small intestine, the stomach has a thicker wall with more collagen and often contains air – attributes that may have a relative protective effect from histotripsy-mediated damage (7). Prior work on the effects of histotripsy on the stomach has been limited to ex vivo treatment of small tissue samples without accounting for blood flow, peristalsis, and stomach contents.

The purpose of this study is to evaluate the degree of damage when histotripsy is performed at the liver capsule using a treatment prescription that includes the gastric wall in the margin in a live human-scale animal model. As the amount of gas in the stomach can vary, treatments were done in both the distended, gas-filled and collapsed condition as a secondary endpoint.

sec.title: Experimental Design (Figure 1)
sec.p: Two hepatic histotripsy treatments (prescribed 2.5 cm diameter spherical treatments) were performed in each of six healthy female pigs (mean weight=52.2 kg, 12 total treatments). The prescribed treatment zones included both the left lateral or medial lobes of the liver and ~0.5 cm diameter of the adjacent stomach wall to mimic inclusion during hepatic histotripsy of surface or exophytic tumors, where ensuring a margin would necessitate extension into the stomach. One treatment per animal was performed with the stomach collapsed and one with the stomach inflated with room air (collapsed treatment first (n=3), inflated first (n=3); 6 treatments in each condition). Immediately post-procedure, animals underwent contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT), euthanasia, and gross and histopathologic evaluation.

sec.title: Animal Handling and Anesthesia
sec.p: This study was approved by the institutional animal care and use committee. Animal sedation, anesthesia maintenance, and euthanasia were performed as previously described (8,9). A 16 Fr orogastric (OG) tube was inserted into the stomach, intragastric positioning confirmed by return of gastric contents or fluoroscopic imaging, and gastric contents removed. The tube was then used to collapse (collapsed condition) or inflate (gas-filled condition) the stomach with room air and clamped for each treatment. For treatments in the gas-filled condition, ultrasound was used to guide and titrate the amount of air insufflation to achieve the desired degree of distention (i.e. gas filled stomach in contact with the liver).

sec.title: Histotripsy Targeting and Treatment
sec.p: Methods for targeting and treatment in swine have been previously described, in short four-cycle pulses are delivered from a 700kHz transducer at 300 pulse repetition frequency (9,10). This method utilizes shock-scattering histotripsy and is equivalent to the method utilized in human treatments. The mean voltage of treatment was recorded for each case. This voltage is increased in each case until cavitation occurs, thus allowing the minimal amount of energy delivery that will allow cavitation to be achieved throughout the treatment volume. This cavitation occurs at after a negative pressure threshold of 26-30 MPa based on prior studies (11). All histotripsy treatments were performed with a commercially available FDA-approved histotripsy system (Edison, HistoSonics, Plymouth, MN) under general endotracheal anesthesia. Animals were positioned supine, and potential treatment locations were initially determined using freehand diagnostic ultrasound (Logiq E10, GE Healthcare, Waukesha WI) (6,12,13). Treatment locations were chosen with the primary intent of including both the liver capsule and ~0.5 cm of adjacent stomach wall within a 2.5 cm diameter spherical prescription, resulting in partial treatment of walls in the collapsed condition and treatment of the full thickness of the thinned wall in the gas-filled condition. To ensure intended treatment of the liver and stomach wall, overlap of treatment margins into the abdominal/chest wall did occur and was tracked, though not germane to the study proper. Additionally, thermal safeguards built into the system, which utilize pauses in treatment to limit damage to overlying structures in the beam path, were bypassed for treatment expediency with resultant skin and abdominal/chest wall damage tracked for completeness, though again beyond the scope of the specific study outcomes (14). Treatment time was 13.5 minutes in each case which is standard for the clinical system creating a 2.5 cm sphere with no cooling time.

sec.title: CT Imaging and Image Review
sec.p: Immediate post-procedure CT scans were performed on a 64-detector helical CT scanner (GE Healthcare Discovery 750 HD, Waukesha, Wisconsin) with scan parameters: 120 kV, 3.75 mm slice thickness, Omnipaque 350 mgI/mL; 2 mL/kg injected at 4 mL/s with scanning during the late arterial and venous phases.

Images were analyzed in consensus by three radiologists: two residents (PGY-4 and PGY-5) and a faculty abdominal and interventional radiologist with 15 years of experience in ablation and post-locoregional therapy image interpretation. Hepatic treatment zones were measured in three dimensions. Additional measurements included the diameter of the treatment zone at the liver capsule adjacent to the stomach and the length of the visible treatment that extended into the stomach wall (Figure 1). Images were evaluated for evidence of gastric perforation, gastric wall thickening, decreased contrast enhancement of the stomach wall, bleeding at the liver capsule, treatment effect in the gastrohepatic mesenteric fat, thrombosis of gastro-mesenteric vessels, and other off-target injuries.

sec.title: Gross Pathology and Histology
sec.p: Animals were euthanized immediately following imaging on the day of treatment and the abdomen inspected for evidence of bleeding, gastric perforation, and off-target injuries. The treated portion of the liver was sliced and photographed, and a sample of the hepatic treatment zone adjacent to the stomach was prepared for histological evaluation. Three samples were taken from each stomach: The 2 treatment zones and a control sample in a distant unaffected region. Samples were fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin and prepared using previously described methods (10,11). Hematoxylin and eosin stained digitally scanned slides were evaluated by a board-certified veterinary pathologist (CWB) with 15 years of experience, including extensive experience with histotripsy. Stomach samples were evaluated for the presence, size, depth, and severity of injury. Since histotripsy was applied to the outer aspects of the stomach wall, the depth of injury was noted as the innermost layer that demonstrated histologic evidence of damage. Severity of injury to the stomach at histopathology was scored on a pre-defined scale from 0 (no injury) to 3 (complete ablation) for cellular injury at each tissue layer with the same scale used for an injury score of the entire stomach wall, where a score of 4 assigned if complete cellular ablation of all tissue layers occurred (Table 1).

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: Confidence intervals (CI) were calculated for estimation of mean observed treatment diameter. A student T-test was used to determine statistically significant differences from the planned diameter at a 5% significance level. Fischer’s exact test was used to evaluate the differences in categorical imaging characteristics between treatment types. Histologic scores were compared using a student’s paired t-test and Wilcoxon signed-rank test. Data was described as mean +/− standard deviation unless otherwise indicated.

sec.title: Procedural
sec.p: All histotripsy treatments were completed as planned (12/12). The mean treatment voltage was 53.5 ± 11.6% of system maximum, with collapsed and gas-filled stomachs treated with mean voltages of 52.0 ± 12.9% and 55.0 ± 11.1%, respectively. Treatment zones were created at a mean depth of 4.7 ± 0.7 cm. Due to the thin lobes of the porcine liver, treatment zones extended into the body wall or to the superficial surface of the liver in 12/12 cases to accommodate the entire 2.5 cm prescribed treatment (Figure 2). For treatments performed in the gas-filled condition (n=6), a mean of 883.3mL ± 495.6 mL of air was used.

sec.title: Treatment sizes (hepatic):
sec.p: Mean hepatic treatment diameters measured 3.2 (0.7) x 2.7 (0.8) x 4.3 (0.8) cm (Anterior-posterior (AP), transverse (TRANS), cranio-caudal (CC)). Mean collapsed treatment diameters measured 3.3 (0.7) x 2.9 (0.7) x 4.7 (0.6) and gas-filled measured 3.1 (0.7) x 2.6 (0.9) x 3.9 (0.8). There was no statistically significant difference in treatment zone size created in the collapsed or gas-filled condition (p=.2749). The mean treatment diameter at the liver capsule adjacent to the targeted stomach wall measured 2.8 ± 1.0 cm with no significant difference between the collapse and gas-filled conditions.

CT evidence of gastric injury was observed in 10/12 treatments. In those cases where a stomach injury was noted, the mean diameter of the injury was 1.7 ± 0.6 cm. The most common imaging findings of gastric injury were decreased enhancement of the gastric wall due to extension of the treatment zone into the stomach and focal wall thickening. There was no imaging evidence of gastric perforation, bleeding at the liver capsule, hemo- or pneumoperitoneum, treatment effect in the gastrohepatic mesenteric fat, thrombosis of gastro-mesenteric vessels, or off-target injuries in any case. The exact depth of the treatment into the stomach could not be accurately discerned by CT.

sec.title: Necropsy/Gross pathology (Table 3)
sec.p: At necropsy, evidence of treatment effect (a focal area of discoloration) in both the liver and the adjacent stomach was evident in all (12/12) cases (Figure 3). When the stomach wall was excised and inspected, hemorrhage (discoloration) was evident on both the serosal and mucosal surface for all treatments (12/12). There was no grossly visible mechanical disruption of the stomach, no leakage of gastric contents, and no hemoperitoneum in any case (0/12). Body wall damage adjacent to the hepatic treatment zone was identified in all cases (12/12) and minor skin injuries were seen in 3/12 (2 small hematomas and 1 area of skin discoloration). There were no off-target injuries to other organs (spleen, kidneys, intestine) detected during necropsy.

sec.title: Stomach:
sec.p: Histolopathologic findings in the gastric wall included mural hemorrhage, and focal ablative effects with destruction of cellular structures in the serosa, muscularis, and mucosa (Figure 4). Mild lymphocytic and granulocytic mural inflammation was present in the ablated region of one animal (cases 1 and 2). Injuries were histologically confirmed as partial (non-transmural) in 11/12 cases, and complete (transmural) in 1/12 cases in a collapsed condition animal (Figure 4). The serosa was injured in all cases (12/12) but was not the deepest extent of injury in any case. The deepest extent of gastric wall injury (from the outer serosal layer to the inner mucosa) for collapsed condition treatments was: Mucosa (n=5), submucosa (n=1), and muscularis (n=0). The deepest extent of gastric wall injury for gas-filled condition treatments was: Mucosa (n=4), submucosa (n=0), and muscularis (n=2). There was no difference in the depth of injury for inflated vs. collapsed stomachs (p>0.05, Wilcoxon Sign Rank test). The severity of injury to the various layers of the gastric wall are summarized in Table 3.

sec.title: Liver:
sec.p: Histologic damage to hepatic tissue adjacent to the stomach was seen in all cases and concordant with prior descriptions of complete hepatic histotripsy treatments (8,9).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Treating capsular or exophytic hepatic tumors by histotripsy will inevitably result in treatment zones that extend into the stomach when an appropriate margin to ensure tumor cellular death is included. While histotripsy has been shown to be highly precise and conformal to the treatment prescription, respiratory or other patient motion may also lead to treatment beyond the prescribed margin which may extend into the stomach wall (11,15). Prior ex vivo work suggests that the stomach can be damaged by sustained transmural histotripsy (7), however histotripsy is delivered in a dynamic environment, with movement of the focal point over time, a likelihood that the gastric wall is only partially included in the periphery of the treatment prescription, and that the gastric wall may move in-and-out of the treatment zone with respiration. This study was designed to simulate this clinical scenario in a live ventilated animal model. The results demonstrate that the stomach can be injured when included in the periphery of the treatment zone, but the majority of injuries were partial thickness of the outer layers (serosa, muscularis) and likely self-limited. In a single case, a transmural injury was detected which had the potential to cause a future gastric perforation. As gas is frequently present in the stomach and could theoretically serve as a seed for cavitation, an important secondary finding was that a gas-filled stomach does not appear to have an effect on the severity of injury.

Gastric injury is a rare but potentially severe complication when focal liver treatments or their radiation effects (radiation therapy, RF, MW, cryoablation, Y90) extend into the stomach (16-22). Radiotherapy has long been known to cause gastric injury, usually manifesting as indigestion, pain, and bleeding, but in severe cases transmural damage can lead to frank perforation (23, 24). Beyond well known off-target embolic effects, Y90 radioembolization has also been associated with gastric injury due to local effects of the high doses of administered radiation (17, 18). The relative paucity of recent reports of gastric injury with thermal ablative therapies is likely due to the widespread adoption of displacement techniques, such as hydrodissection, increasing physician experience, and the relative thickness of the gastric wall (19-21). In the case of histotripsy, adjunctive protective maneuvers such as hydrodissection, air insufflation into the peritoneal space, and displacement with balloons or needle leverage techniques are all feasible, but would convert histotripsy from a non-invasive to a minimally invasive procedure (25), add time and complexity, potentially degrade the ultrasound window, and may not be necessary based on the mechanism of action.

This is the first study of histotripsy applied to the stomach in a live animal model. Prior ex vivo studies of histotripsy applied to bowel have demonstrated that stomach and small bowel can be injured when directly targeted with sustained cavitation at a single site (7). In this study, the majority of the gastric injuries were not severe, likely due to the highly precise nature of histotripsy, treatments extending into peripheral layers of a collapsed stomach, and limited cavitation within the relatively thin walls of a gas-filled stomach. With needle-based or therapeutic ultrasound thermal ablative modalities, a thermal gradient is produced at the probe tip or focal point (26, 27), and in the absence of intervening fluid or air, there is no barrier to the transmission of heat into adjacent tissues. In contrast, the mechanism of action with histotripsy is cavitation, which is not thermal and does not spread into adjacent tissues (11). Additional reasons for the relative lack of severe injuries include the higher cavitation threshold of the stomach compared to liver (7), and respiratory and peristaltic motion that may move the targeted portion of the stomach in-and-out of the treatment prescription.

The question as to whether intraluminal air is protective or exacerbates gastric injuries remains unclear based on the results of this study, though the presence of gas within and adjacent to the treatment zone does not worsen these injuries. A collapsed organ without gas may predispose the stomach to damage by histotripsy due to an unobstructed sonic window and provide a more appropriate matrix for initiation of cavitation, though also allows excellent visualization for precise targeting. A gas-filled stomach thins the wall, where the full thickness of the wall is within the treatment prescription, theoretically increasing risk of full thickness injury, though interestingly full-thickness injury was not seen in any animal treated with a gas-filled stomach in this study. A gas-filled stomach does increase blockage of the ultrasound beam which is potentially protective of cavitation initiation, though also presents a highly complex sonic environment with varying degrees of visualization, motion, blockage, and tissue inhomogeneity which can create aberration (altering the focal point), and varying cavitation thresholds which may increase the potential for incomplete treatments (28, 29). Further, from a clinical standpoint for a patient under general anesthesia, it is desirable to keep the stomach decompressed to minimize anesthesia related risks like aspiration. This question is difficult to fully explore in a normal swine model where sample sizes are limited and there are no tumors. The question could be further studied in a tumor-bearing model to determine if adequate cavitation for tumor destruction can occur in the immediate vicinity of a gas-filled structure. Simulations may also allow further evaluation of the complex interplay of blockage, motion, and tissue heterogeneity caused by treatment immediately adjacent to gas-filled structures.

This study had several limitations. The acute nature of the study and lack of a survival period did not allow for the evaluation of the integrity of the stomach wall over time. However, histopathology is known to be an adequate surrogate for the clinical significance of a particular gastric injury (30, 31). An additional limitation is that histotripsy treatments were performed on healthy swine livers without cirrhosis (32, 33), steatosis (34), or tumors due to the cost and complexity associated with these models (35). Another limitation was the lack of intermediate states of gastric insufflation. Only two extreme scenarios were tested: collapsed and gas-filled. Thus, the results in other states of inflation are not known. Finally, only the outer 5 mm of the gastric wall were targeted for histotripsy treatment in the collapsed state. This may underestimate the amount of damage that can occur in the event of catastrophic mistargeting in which the entire wall of a collapsed stomach is included in the treatment prescription. Direct targeting of the stomach would have been an alternate experimental design and may have increased the degree of gastric injury, but would not be a likely clinical scenario and thus was not explored in this study.

In summary, the results of this study demonstrate that inclusion of the stomach wall in the outer 5 mm margin of a left hepatic lobe histotripsy treatment generally results in limited, partial thickness injuries that are unlikely to result in gastric perforation. The degree of injury is not worsened when the stomach is gas-filled, despite the full thickness of the wall being in the treatment prescription. Caution should be maintained, though, as full thickness injuries are possible and treating physicians should minimize the amount of stomach wall included in histotripsy treatment prescriptions to the extent possible.

ack.p: Research support for K.L.F. in this publication was provided by the National Cancer Institute of the National Institutes of Health under Award Number 1F30CA28454101A1 and by the National Institute of General Medicine Sciences under award T32GM140935. Figures were created with BioRender under license numbers XU28BC76RH, ES28BC76UK, BU28BC76XE.

sec.title: DATA AVAILIBILITY STATEMENT:
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author, (TJZ), upon reasonable request.

WangL. Establishment of a hepatic cirrhosis and portal hypertension model by hepatic arterial perfusion with 80% alcohol. World J Gastroenterol. 2015;21(32):9544. doi: 10.3748/wjg.v21.i32.9544.26327762 PMC4548115

AvritscherR, WrightKC, JavadiS, Development of a Large Animal Model of Cirrhosis and Portal Hypertension Using Hepatic Transarterial Embolization: A Study in Swine. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2011;22(9):1329–1334. doi: 10.1016/j.jvir.2011.04.016.21802316

HvidH, HjulerST, BedossaP, Choline-deficient, high-fat diet-induced MASH in Göttingen Minipigs: characterization and effects of a chow reversal period. Am J Physiol-Gastrointest Liver Physiol. 2024;327(4):G571–G585. doi: 10.1152/ajpgi.00120.2024.39041677 PMC11482250
Litten-BrownJC, CorsonAM, ClarkeL. Porcine models for the metabolic syndrome, digestive and bone disorders: a general overview. Animal. 2010;4(6):899–920. doi: 10.1017/S1751731110000200.22444262

caption.p: Experimental design (left) and schematic (right) demonstrating the location of treatment zones and measurements. Each prescribed histotripsy treatments consisted of 2.5 cm diameter spherical zones. The green dotted line indicates how the stomach was measured for injury due to treatment, and the blue dotted line indicate how the liver capsule was measured for injury due to treatment.

A swine with two treatments with both collapsed and gas-filled stomach. Ultrasound images demonstrating targeting (a, b) of the treatment zone in the liver (T) including the stomach (S) posteriorly. Note that the treatment zone partially extends into the stomach wall when collapsed, while spanning the entire stomach wall when gas-filled. Post-treatment contrast-enhanced CT corresponding to the targeted zones (c,d).
Gross pathology images. (a) Photograph of two treatment zones (arrows) in one swine with corresponding (b,c) gastric hemorrhage (blue circles) located posteriorly on the stomach serosa.

(a,b) Representative gross and histopathological images of a minor injury to the stomach after histotripsy liver treatment. Gross image (a) demonstrating minor stomach injury (blue circle) adjacent to the treatment zone within the liver. Representative histologic example (b) of a stomach injury score of 1, minimal injury with minor microscopic hemorrhage (arrow, bar=3mm). (c,d,e) Gross and histopathologic images of a severe injury to the stomach after treatment. Serosal (c) and mucosal (d) surfaces of the stomach demonstrating hemorrhagic injury. Corresponding histopathology (e) of the stomach and pylorus with extensive ablation effect within the serosa, muscularis and submucosa with extension into the mucosa. The low magnification inset encompasses the extent of the ablated region (brackets, bar=3mm). The high magnification image (black box on inset) highlights regions of necrosis with karyorrhectic debris and eosinophilic material with loss of cellular and nuclear detail (asteriks) throughout the serosa, muscularis and submucosa. There are scant foci of necrosis in the interstitium of the mucosa (arrowheads) with amphophilic to basophilic amorphous debris, while the mucosal foveolar epithelium and Brunner’s gland epithelial cells are histologically intact.
Ablation and Injury Score for Histology

tr.td: Minimal injury to tissue layer/Minimal injury to stomach wall
Severe injury to tissue layer/Severe injury to stomach wall

td.bold: Diameter of treatment zone on hepatic capsule (cm)

fn.p: p>.05 and not considered statistically significant
p<.05 and considered to be statistically significant

caption.p: Pathologic Findings of Stomach Post Histotripsy
Summary of Gross Pathology and Histology (stomach)
